s: The world's only swing aqueduct — a canal crossing a canal since 1894
w: Designed by Edward Leader Williams and opened in 1894, this 234-metre iron trough pivots on a central island to let ships through the Manchester Ship Canal below while narrowboats float across it above. The only structure of its kind on earth. It replaced a fixed stone aqueduct that James Brindley built in 1761, the first in England. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Designed by Sir Edward Leader Williams, opened 1894; built by Andrew Handyside and Company of Derby
The only swing aqueduct in the world — it carries the Bridgewater Canal over the Manchester Ship Canal
Pivots on a central island to allow large vessels through the ship canal below
Replaced the 1761 Barton Aqueduct designed by James Brindley — the first navigable aqueduct in England
Grade II* listed building

s: England's second crematorium, 1892 — Lombard-Romanesque beside the Smiths' cemetery
w: When it opened in 1892, Manchester Crematorium was only the second in Britain — the first in Woking had opened a mere six years earlier, still amid fierce public opposition. Architects Steinthal and Solomons chose a grave Lombard-Romanesque style to make the unfamiliar seem ancient and trustworthy. It sits adjacent to Southern Cemetery, which Morrissey haunted as a teenager.
_facts: Opened 1892 as the second crematorium in the United Kingdom
Designed by architects Steinthal and Solomons in the Lombard-Romanesque style
Located on Barlow Moor Road, Chorlton-cum-Hardy, adjacent to Southern Cemetery
The first UK crematorium opened in Woking in 1885 amid considerable public controversy

s: Sold for £1 after the World Monuments Fund listed it alongside Pompeii
w: Edward Welby Pugin's Gothic Revival masterpiece for the Franciscan friars, built 1866-1872, closed for worship in 1989, then stripped by developers who went bankrupt. By 1997 it was on the World Monuments Fund Watch List alongside Pompeii and the Taj Mahal. Purchased for £1, its twelve limestone saints recovered from a Sotheby's auction labelled 'garden ornaments.' Now a wedding venue and concert hall.
_facts: Designed by Edward Welby Pugin, built 1866-1872 for Belgian Recollect Franciscans; closed 1989
In 1997 placed on the World Monuments Fund Watch List alongside Pompeii and the Taj Mahal
Purchased for £1 by a charitable trust led by Elaine and Paul Griffiths in 1997
Twelve limestone Franciscan saint statues were found listed as 'garden ornaments' at Sotheby's in 1994 and reinstated in 2012
Restoration completed 2007 using £6 million from the Heritage Lottery Fund; now an events and weddings venue

s: Van Gogh, Picasso, and Gauguin were stolen here — found rolled in a public toilet
w: Founded in 1889 with a bequest from Sir Joseph Whitworth, this gallery in Whitworth Park holds over 60,000 works — the greatest collection of wallpapers and textiles anywhere. In April 2003, three works worth £4 million including Van Gogh's 'The Fortification of Paris' were stolen. They turned up in a nearby public toilet, rolled inside a tube, with a note claiming the theft was to highlight poor security.
_facts: Founded 1889 from a bequest by Sir Joseph Whitworth; building opened 1908; part of the University of Manchester since 1958
Collection exceeds 60,000 items including the finest collection of wallpapers and textiles in the world
In April 2003, three paintings by Van Gogh, Picasso, and Gauguin worth £4 million were stolen
The paintings were found rolled inside a cardboard tube in a nearby public toilet with a note about security
Won the Art Fund Museum of the Year award in 2015 following a £15 million redevelopment

s: A 150-tonne space pod floats inside Manchester's cotton exchange hall
w: The Great Hall of the Royal Exchange — once the world's largest trading room, echoing with cotton prices — now contains a seven-sided steel-and-glass theatre module suspended from four columns because the floor couldn't bear its weight. The 150-tonne 'space pod' seats 800 in the round. The 1996 IRA bomb moved the dome; the theatre reopened in 1998 with the play that should have opened on the day of the blast.
_facts: Royal Exchange building dates to 1729; current Great Hall from 1874, extended 1921
The theatre module, designed by Richard Negri and opened 1976, weighs 150 tonnes and is suspended from four columns because the trading-floor could not bear its load
Largest theatre-in-the-round in the world, seating around 800 across three levels
On 15 June 1996 an IRA bomb on nearby Corporation Street caused the dome to shift; repairs cost £32 million
Reopened 30 November 1998 with 'Hindle Wakes' — the play scheduled to open the day of the bombing

s: The concert hall that floats on 280 steel springs to silence the Metrolink
w: Home of the Halle since 1996, Bridgewater Hall sits above the Metrolink tram line — and solved the noise problem by mounting its 26,500-tonne superstructure on 280 GERB steel-spring isolation bearings, the first concert hall in the world built this way. The auditorium is essentially a building within a building, floating free of the city's vibrations.
_facts: Opened 1996 as permanent home of the Halle Orchestra; cost approximately £42 million
The 26,500-tonne superstructure rests on 280 GERB isolation bearings — rows of steel springs between concrete piers
First concert hall in the world to be built using this seismic isolation technology
The system insulates the auditorium from noise and vibration from the adjacent Metrolink tram line and road
Reinforced concrete auditorium described as 'moulded and cast like a vast sculpture'

s: The only surviving electric Manchester tram, running on a line buried since the 1930s
w: Underneath the asphalt of Heaton Park lay a 1903 Manchester Corporation Tramways spur, buried when trams were scrapped in the 1930s. Volunteers dug it out, and the line reopened in 1980. Tram 765, built in 1914, is the last electric tramcar from Manchester's original network still running. It departs on Sunday afternoons, 0.52 miles across the largest municipal park in Greater Manchester.
_facts: Restored heritage tramway opened 28 March 1980, operated by Manchester Transport Museum Society
The route follows a 1903 Manchester Corporation Tramways branch line that was buried under asphalt in the 1930s
Manchester 765 (1914) is the only remaining electric tramcar from Manchester's original tram network in regular operation
The 0.52-mile line runs through Heaton Park, Greater Manchester's largest park at over 600 acres
Fleet of 14 vehicles; operates Sunday afternoons March to mid-November, Saturday afternoons May to mid-September

s: 750-year-old manor where, legend says, the Gunpowder Plot was hatched
w: The oldest surviving secular building in Greater Manchester, Ordsall Hall has stood here for over 750 years, the seat of the Radclyffe family for three centuries. A local legend — unsubstantiated but persistent — holds that the 1605 Gunpowder Plot was planned in this hall, a story William Harrison Ainsworth put into his 1842 novel 'Guy Fawkes.' The Tudor Great Hall and its carved timberwork are intact.
_facts: Origins date back over 750 years; oldest surviving parts of the present hall built in the 15th century
Family seat of the Radclyffe family for more than 300 years
Local legend, described as 'plausible although unsubstantiated,' holds that the Gunpowder Plot of 1605 was planned here
William Harrison Ainsworth used the legend in his 1842 novel 'Guy Fawkes'
Now a museum managed by Salford City Council; Grade I listed

s: A Victorian police station frozen in 1879, with its cells still intact
w: Newton Street Police Station opened in 1879 and served Manchester City Police and its successors for exactly a century. When it closed in 1979, the interior was preserved rather than gutted: the station office, Victorian cells, and the charge room are all intact, redesigned to evoke the 1890s. Now a free museum and archive of policing history, Grade II listed.
_facts: Former Newton Street Police Station, built 1879, served Manchester City Police until 1979
Interior redesigned to reflect Victorian-era policing after conversion to museum in 1981
Grade II listed building (designated 1994) at 57A Newton Street in the Northern Quarter
Holds archives and artefacts documenting Greater Manchester policing history

s: The RSPB was born in this garden in 1889, to stop women wearing dead birds
w: In 1889 Emily Williamson, whose husband Robert was a botanist who owned this garden, formed the Plumage League in The Croft — a protest against the fashion for wearing exotic bird feathers on hats. That small meeting became the Royal Society for the Protection of Birds. The garden was donated to Manchester in 1919 by alderman Fletcher Moss, whose 90 acres now hold a walled rock garden, giant Gunnera, and palm trees.
_facts: The Croft within the garden hosted the inaugural meeting of what became the RSPB in 1889, founded by Emily Williamson to protest bird-killing fashion
Garden and rock garden purchased by botanist Robert Wood Williamson; donated to Manchester in 1919 by alderman Fletcher Moss
Walled south-facing rock garden shelters non-hardy species including Chusan palms and dawn redwoods
90-acre park adjacent to the River Mersey in Didsbury; Green Flag Award status since 2000

s: Alfred Waterhouse's radial star prison, with a 234-foot ventilation tower mistaken for a watchtower
w: The same Alfred Waterhouse who built Manchester Town Hall also designed this prison, opened in 1869 at a cost of £170,000. Its plan is a star or snowflake — ten wings radiating from a central core — and its 234-foot tower, often mistaken for a watchtower, is actually a ventilation chimney. In April 1990 prisoners seized the roof for 25 days in the longest prison riot in British penal history.
_facts: Designed by Alfred Waterhouse and completed 1869, cost £170,000; Grade II listed
Radial 'separate system' plan with ten wings radiating from a central core, shaped like a star
The 234-foot tower is a ventilation structure, not a watchtower, and has become a Manchester landmark
The 1990 Strangeways riot lasted 25 days (1-25 April), the longest prison riot in British penal history; 147 staff and 47 prisoners injured
Renamed HM Prison Manchester after rebuilding completed in 1994

s: Grand 1880 terminus with the UK's second-longest station roof — spent years as a car park
w: Opened in 1880 as the Cheshire Lines Committee's terminus, Manchester Central has the second-longest single-span railway roof in the UK at 64 metres. After closing to passengers in 1969, it became a car park, then in October 1965 police discovered evidence in a left-luggage locker linking Ian Brady and Myra Hindley to the Moors murders. Converted to G-MEX in 1986, now the city's main convention centre.
_facts: Opened 1 July 1880; architect Sir John Fowler designed the 550-foot long, 64-metre span wrought iron roof — second-longest in the UK
Closed to passengers 5 May 1969 and subsequently used as a car park for many years
In October 1965, evidence in a left-luggage locker helped link Ian Brady and Myra Hindley to the Moors murders
Converted to exhibition centre G-MEX, opening 1986; now Manchester Central Convention Complex
Grade II* listed building

s: Oak sculpture marking where the purple drink was invented in 1908
w: John Noel Nichols invented Vimto in Manchester in 1908 and ran his business from Granby Row. Kerry Morrison's 1992 oak sculpture — a giant bottle surrounded by the fruits of the original recipe — marks the spot. Restored and repainted in 2011, it sits outside the university precinct and is quietly one of Manchester's stranger monuments.
_facts: Vimto invented in 1908 by John Noel Nichols of Blackburn, who operated from premises on Granby Row, Manchester
Oak sculpture 'A Monument to Vimto' created by Kerry Morrison and installed on Granby Row in 1992
The sculpture depicts a giant Vimto bottle surrounded by grapes, raspberries, and blackcurrants
Restored and repainted in 2011; production later moved to a warehouse on Chapel Street, Salford

s: Tudor manor besieged by Roundheads in 1644, cannon-fire ending a four-month standoff
w: Robert Tatton and his Royalist household held out in this 16th-century timber-framed manor through the winter of 1643-44 until the Parliamentarians brought two cannons from Manchester. They surrendered on 27 February 1644. The Tattons recovered the estate — paying a fine of just over £700 — and remained for nearly four centuries in total. Now free to visit in Wythenshawe Park.
_facts: 16th-century timber-framed manor house built for Robert Tatton; the family's seat for nearly 400 years
Besieged by Parliamentarian forces under Robert Duckenfield in winter 1643-44 during the English Civil War
Royalists surrendered 27 February 1644 after Parliamentarians brought two cannons from Manchester
Tattons recovered the estate by paying a fine of just over £700 after the Civil War
Now a free museum managed by Manchester City Council, set within Wythenshawe Park

s: Alfred Waterhouse's 1895 insurance palace, where women had to reapply if they married
w: Alfred Waterhouse's red-brick and terracotta pile was built 1891-1895 for the Refuge Assurance Company, which eventually employed 2,000 staff here. Women were required to reapply for their jobs upon marriage; men and women ate in segregated areas. The basement ballroom became a lunchtime dance hall. The 217-foot tower — added in 1910-12 by Waterhouse's son Paul — is now the hotel's name. Said to be haunted by a grieving war widow.
_facts: Designed by Alfred Waterhouse for the Refuge Assurance Company; main building completed 1895
Grade II* listed; the 217-foot clock tower added 1910-12 by Paul Waterhouse
Around 2,000 staff worked here; women were required to reapply for jobs upon marriage
Basement contained a segregated canteen that later became a lunchtime dance hall for employees
Converted to the Palace Hotel in 1996; now the Kimpton Clocktower Hotel

s: Lutyens memorial built above Victorian burial crypts that nobody could move
w: Sir Edwin Lutyens designed this 32-tonne Portland stone cenotaph, unveiled in 1924 — Manchester was unusually late, not convening a memorial committee until 1922. The site had a problem: sealed burial crypts from the demolished St Peter's Church lay beneath it, and legal trustees refused their removal. Lutyens built around them. When Metrolink expanded across the square in 2014, the crypts were simply given a concrete lid.
_facts: Designed by Sir Edwin Lutyens, unveiled 12 July 1924 by the 17th Earl of Derby; cost £6,940
Manchester's war memorial committee was not convened until 1922, unusually late
St Peter's Square was the site of St Peter's Church (demolished 1907); sealed burial crypts remained underground
Legal trustees refused removal of the crypts; Lutyens designed the cenotaph to co-exist with them
When Metrolink expanded across the square in 2014, the crypts were provided with a concrete lid and left in place

s: Former dockland, inaccessible island — the Metrolink's least-used stop for years
w: Pomona Docks closed in 1982, unlike Salford Quays they were never regenerated, and the land became a mysterious brownfield island between the Manchester Ship Canal and the Bridgewater Canal. A Metrolink tram stop opened here in 1999 — for years the least-used on the entire network, a platform in a field of industrial scrub. Now slowly surrounded by new apartments; the emptiness is ending.
_facts: Pomona Docks consisted of five dock basins opened 1894 as part of the Port of Manchester, closed 1982
Unlike nearby Salford Quays, the docks were never redeveloped, leaving a brownfield peninsula
Pomona Metrolink stop opened December 1999; for the majority of its operational years it was the least-used stop on the entire network
The swing roadbridge separating Salford Quays from Pomona is now permanently closed
Residential development began in the late 2010s; Trafford Park Line extended through in 2020

s: Widest nave in England, medieval misericords — and the earliest known backgammon image in Britain
w: Claimed to have the widest nave of any English cathedral, Manchester Cathedral has thirty 16th-century misericords considered among the finest in Europe. One — N-08 — carries what is believed to be the earliest known depiction of backgammon in the UK. The carved roof angels each play a different late medieval instrument: lute, hurdy-gurdy, bagpipes — all secular, none ecclesiastical. Grade I listed, originally collegiate.
_facts: Commonly claimed to have the widest nave of any cathedral in England
Thirty 16th-century misericords, 'considered to be among the finest in Europe'
Misericord N-08 is believed to be the earliest known depiction of backgammon in the UK
Fourteen life-size angel sculptures in the nave roof each play a different late medieval secular instrument including lute, hurdy-gurdy, and bagpipes
Grade I listed; originally a collegiate church, became a cathedral in 1847

s: The UK's largest municipal cemetery — Tony Wilson's headstone designed by Peter Saville
w: Opened in 1879 on 100 acres and expanded to 190, Southern Cemetery is the largest municipal cemetery in the UK and the second largest in Europe. John Rylands is buried here, as is Matt Busby, L.S. Lowry, and John Alcock — pilot of the first non-stop transatlantic flight. Tony Wilson's headstone was designed by Peter Saville. The Smiths named a song after the cemetery gates.
_facts: Opened 1879; expanded 1926; now 190 acres — the largest municipal cemetery in the UK and second-largest in Europe
Notable burials include John Rylands, Matt Busby, L.S. Lowry, Tony Wilson (headstone designed by Peter Saville), and John Alcock (first non-stop transatlantic flight, 1919)
The Smiths released 'Cemetry Gates' (1986) referencing the cemetery; Morrissey walked through it as a teenager
Contains 775 Commonwealth WWI graves and 475 WWII graves

s: Each of its five floors is a different architectural style — saved from the Blitz by its own textiles
w: Built 1851-56 for textile wholesalers S&J Watts, this was the largest single-occupancy warehouse in Manchester. Architect Travis and Magnell gave each floor a different style — Italian Renaissance, Elizabethan, French Renaissance, Flemish — topped with Gothic wheel windows. In the Blitz a Luftwaffe bomb struck but was smothered by the textiles inside. James Watts entertained Prince Albert here in 1857. Now the Britannia Hotel.
_facts: Built 1851-1856 by architects Travis & Magnell for S&J Watts at a cost of £100,000
The largest single-occupancy textile warehouse in Manchester at the time of construction
The five-storey facade displays five different architectural styles: Venetian palazzo, Italian Renaissance, Elizabethan, French Renaissance, and Flemish, with Gothic wheel windows at the roofline
During WWII a Luftwaffe bomb struck the building but the fire was smothered by the textiles stored inside
James Watts entertained Prince Albert during his 1857 visit; converted to the Britannia Hotel in 1982
